upperAir: The KOAK sounding shows a remarkably stable atmosphere with a near-zero lapse rate from surface to 850mb (12.6°C at surface, 12.6°C at 1536m) — this is essentially an isothermal layer indicating a strong low-level inversion capping thermals near 1500m AGL. Above 850mb lapse rates improve but remain sub-adiabatic. The extremely dry air aloft (39°C spread at 850mb) confirms no convective moisture, and light winds (4–6 kts) throughout the column mean minimal wind shear but also very weak mechanical forcing.
aviationFlags: No active AIRMETs for the Bay Area SF region. METARs are benign: KRHV reporting SCT080 with calm to light winds and warm temps (27–29°C), KHAF CLR with light variable winds and warm temps (20–22°C) indicating no marine layer intrusion at the coast this afternoon. KSFO calm with BKN200 (high thin cloud). KAPC reporting 120/11kt and 26°C, suggesting a modest onshore gradient developing. KWVI calm and warm at 28°C. Overall clean flying weather with no hazard advisories.

sites.name: Ed Levin (1750 ft)
sites.howItWorks: Ed Levin high launch works best on thermal days with a valley breeze cycling S to SW 8–15 kts by midday. Thermals develop off the sun-baked Diablo Range and can carry pilots to 3000–5000 ft MSL on good days. Sea breeze arrival from the west can cycle the wind and create punchy conditions later in the afternoon.
sites.todaySetup: Today is a marginal thermal day at Ed Levin high launch due to the strong isothermal inversion capping the boundary layer near 1500m (~4900 ft MSL), which will limit thermal height gain even as surface heating proceeds. The good news: skies are clear, winds are light and variable from the south (SSW 3–5 kts at the surface through midday), and BL depth builds from ~335m at 10 AM to ~1180–1285m by 2–4 PM — sufficient for moderate thermals to the inversion. However, the cap means thermals will be weak-to-moderate and truncated, not punchy. The afternoon wind shift to NNW around 5–6 PM (354°/5.5 kts at 18Z) signals the sea breeze arriving, which can cycle the bowl and create cross/off-wind conditions at launch. Cloud cover spikes to 76% at 3 PM then 100% at 7 PM, suggesting mid-afternoon sky cover that could kill thermals early. Best window is the midday thermal cycle before the sea breeze intrudes.

hourlyWindows.label: Morning (8–11 AM)
hourlyWindows.summary: Light and variable winds 0–5 kts, mostly SSE/S; BL only 45–485m. Thermals just initiating by 10–11 AM. Marginal for soaring, more suitable for sledder assessment.

hourlyWindows.label: Midday (11 AM–2 PM)
hourlyWindows.summary: BL builds to 940–1180m by noon-2 PM; surface winds SSW 2–5 kts — cleanest window for thermal flying. Expect weak-to-moderate thermals capped near 4000–4500 ft MSL.

hourlyWindows.label: Afternoon (2 PM–sunset)
hourlyWindows.summary: BL peaks ~1285m around 4 PM but cloud cover surges (76% at 3 PM, 50–100% by 6 PM) and wind shifts NNW 5–6 kts by 6 PM. Thermals dying as sky cover increases; sea breeze cycling the bowl. Exit before 5 PM.

verdict.hazards: Strong inversion caps thermals ~4500 ft MSL; afternoon wind shift NNW + increasing cloud cover by 3 PM; light winds make it hard to stay up if thermals cycle off

sites.name: Ed Levin (600 ft)
sites.howItWorks: The 600 ft training hill at Ed Levin is a pure thermal site with a short sledder when conditions are weak. It works best when valley thermals are punchy enough to climb from the low launch, typically needing BL heights above 400–600m and south winds 5–12 kts. It is more forgiving than the high launch but also less rewarding.
sites.todaySetup: The 600 ft launch has a very similar story to the high launch: a stable inversion caps thermals well below their ideal height, but the lower launch means even modest BL development (280–1020m by midday) could support scratchy soaring or extended sledders. Surface winds are light and variable SSW through midday at 3–5 kts. The site is usable for P2 pilots looking for a mellow thermal day, but with a BL only marginally deeper than launch height in the morning, expect mostly sledder conditions until 11 AM+. Cloud buildup after 3 PM and the NNW shift at 6 PM apply here too. The low launch is a reasonable option if the high launch is too intimidating on the light-wind day.

hourlyWindows.label: Morning (8–11 AM)
hourlyWindows.summary: Very light winds (2–5 kts SSE/S); BL 45–435m — marginal for soaring. Expect mostly sledders with occasional thermal punches.

hourlyWindows.label: Midday (11 AM–2 PM)
hourlyWindows.summary: BL grows to 405–1020m; winds SSW 4–5 kts. Best window for scratchy soaring off the training hill. Thermals will be light but present.

hourlyWindows.label: Afternoon (2 PM–sunset)
hourlyWindows.summary: BL peaks ~1130–1285m but clouds increase significantly after 3 PM (77% at 3 PM). Wind shifts WNW then NNW by evening. Wrap up by 4–5 PM.

verdict.hazards: Inversion caps thermals; cloud buildup kills heating after 3 PM; evening NNW wind shift; very light winds may produce extended sledders

sites.name: Mussel Rock
sites.locationDescriptor: Daly City — coastal bluff, W/NW-facing ridge lift site
sites.howItWorks: Mussel Rock is a pure ridge soaring site driven by the onshore marine/sea breeze from the W-NW. Ideal winds are 10–20 kts from 270–320°. The site fires when the gradient pushes marine air over the coastal bluff; too light and there's no lift, too strong and it becomes survival flying. Rotor off the bluff edge and bullet thermal cycles are the main hazards.
sites.todaySetup: Mussel Rock has a split-personality day today. The morning starts with an ESE offshore flow (110° at 7–9 kts through 11 AM), which is completely wrong direction for ridge lift and would put pilots in rotor/lee conditions — do not fly in this window. The wind then goes notably light and variable (2–3 kts) between 2–5 PM as the flow transitions, before shifting WNW/NW by evening (300°+ at 3–6 kts, rising to 6+ kts). The afternoon NW shift is in the right direction but at only 2–4 kts surface it is too light for reliable ridge lift. The 80m wind shows 3–4 kts from WNW in the evening, still marginal. KHAF METARs confirm no marine layer — the coast is clear and warm (20–22°C), consistent with a weak, late-developing onshore push. No AIRMETs, buoys show 1.5–1.6m swell at 9s and light westerly winds offshore. The morning offshore flow is the showstopper; the afternoon transition might produce brief flyable conditions but the window is narrow and unreliable.

hourlyWindows.label: Morning (8–11 AM)
hourlyWindows.summary: ESE offshore flow at 8–10 kts (105–110°) — completely wrong for ridge lift, creates lee rotor off the bluff. Do not fly this window.

hourlyWindows.label: Midday (11 AM–2 PM)
hourlyWindows.summary: Wind direction shifts and weakens dramatically to SE/variable 2–7 kts. Transitional and unreliable; BL collapses from ridge-lift perspective. Avoid.

hourlyWindows.label: Afternoon (2 PM–sunset)
hourlyWindows.summary: Flow begins shifting to WNW/NW 300°+ at 3–6 kts by 4–6 PM — correct direction but likely too light for sustained ridge lift. Potentially soarable for 30–60 min if the gradient fills in, but not reliable.

verdict.wind: ESE 8–10 kts AM; WNW 3–4 kts PM
verdict.hazards: Morning ESE offshore flow creates rotor off the bluff; afternoon onshore shift too light for ridge lift; transitional direction all day; narrow potential window at dusk is not worth the drive

sites.name: Mission Peak
sites.locationDescriptor: Fremont — inland ridge, SE-facing thermal/ridge site
sites.howItWorks: Mission Peak launches work on thermal days with a SW–SE valley breeze and BL development above 700–1000m. The site benefits from strong inland heating and can produce robust thermals on spring days, but the low-level inversion today and variable surface winds limit potential. Best on days with steady S–SW flow and good sun.
sites.todaySetup: Mission Peak today is in essentially the same atmospheric environment as Ed Levin — the same strong isothermal inversion at 850mb caps thermals near 4500–5000 ft MSL, and surface winds are light and variable SSW through midday. BL development follows the same pattern: slow start (45–335m by 10 AM), building to 940–1180m by noon-2 PM. The wind data at Mission Peak is nearly identical to Ed Levin, suggesting a uniform inland valley pattern. Surface winds of 2–5 kts SSW are usable for launch but won't provide reliable ridge assist. The afternoon cloud surge (76% at 3 PM, rising to 100% by 7 PM) is the same concern, killing the thermal cycle before sunset. The evening NNW shift at 354°/5.5 kts is also mirrored here. Overall a marginal thermal day with a limited midday window, similar in quality to Ed Levin — but Mission Peak's steeper terrain and more exposed ridge can make transitional winds more problematic.

hourlyWindows.label: Morning (8–11 AM)
hourlyWindows.summary: Light winds 0–5 kts variable/SSE; BL only 45–485m. Thermals just getting started by 10–11 AM. Mostly a sledder morning.

hourlyWindows.label: Midday (11 AM–2 PM)
hourlyWindows.summary: BL climbs to 940–1180m; surface winds SSW 2–5 kts. Best thermal window of the day — moderate thermals capped near 4000–4500 ft MSL. Soarable but not epic.

hourlyWindows.label: Afternoon (2 PM–sunset)
hourlyWindows.summary: Cloud cover surges to 76% by 3 PM and 100% by 7 PM, cutting thermal production. Wind shifts NNW 5–6 kts by 6 PM. Land by 4:30–5 PM at the latest.

verdict.hazards: Isothermal inversion truncates thermals; afternoon cloud cover kills heating; evening NNW wind shift; light winds may make staying up challenging

tomorrow.name: Ed Levin (1750 ft)
tomorrow.analysis: Sunday looks problematic — 100% cloud cover all day per both GFS and HRRR, with BL development limited to 325–430m in the morning and only reaching ~1130m by 2 PM under overcast skies. Light variable winds throughout. Thermals will be weak at best under the cloud deck. Not recommended unless skies clear unexpectedly.

tomorrow.name: Ed Levin (600 ft)
tomorrow.analysis: Same overcast story as the high launch — 100% cloud cover through most of the day. BL barely builds to 180m by 10 AM. Training hill sledders only, and even those may be unreliable without thermal triggers under solid overcast.

tomorrow.name: Mussel Rock
tomorrow.analysis: Tomorrow looks even lighter onshore with 100% cloud cover persisting all day per GFS/HRRR. Winds shift to NW/WNW 5–9 kts by afternoon-evening — correct direction but marginal strength under solid marine overcast. Tomorrow.io shows ESE 6 kts in the morning again. Not a reliable fly day; watch for possible evening ridge window if the overcast thins.

tomorrow.name: Mission Peak
tomorrow.analysis: Overcast and light winds mirror Ed Levin — 100% cloud cover, BL barely building, light variable winds all day. Very poor thermal day. Skip Sunday.

day2.name: Ed Levin (1750 ft)
day2.analysis: Monday (Apr 6) looks significantly better — clearing skies (0% cloud by noon), BL building to 1060m by 2 PM, and NW winds 8–9 kts at the surface by afternoon. A more classic spring pattern with a proper sea breeze and better thermal development. Watch the NW flow — could be punchy.

day2.name: Ed Levin (600 ft)
day2.analysis: Monday improves at the lower launch too — clear skies, BL to 745–1060m, and NW/WNW winds 5–8 kts. Better thermal day than today or tomorrow. P2-friendly if the NW gradient isn't too strong.

day2.name: Mussel Rock
day2.analysis: Monday looks much better for Mussel Rock — WSW/W flow at 8–9 kts (261–293°) all day with 80m winds at 10–11 kts and clearing skies by midday. A solid ridge soaring day in the making. P3+ for the morning, potentially P2+ by afternoon if wind moderates slightly.

day2.name: Mission Peak
day2.analysis: Monday also improves at Mission Peak — clear skies, BL to 885m by 2 PM, and WSW/NW winds 4–6 kts at the surface with stronger flow aloft (7–10 kts at 80m). Better thermal and ridge day. P2+ with good potential midday-afternoon window.

day3.name: Ed Levin (1750 ft)
day3.analysis: No day 3 (Apr 7) data available in current forecast window. Extrapolate from the improving trend on Monday — if the NW gradient holds, Tuesday could be another solid inland thermal day.

day3.name: Ed Levin (600 ft)
day3.analysis: Same as above — trend appears improving but no additional data beyond Apr 6 in the dataset.

day3.name: Mussel Rock
day3.analysis: If Monday's WSW pattern holds, Tuesday at Mussel Rock could be another ridge soaring day. Monitor the gradient strength as the synoptic pattern evolves.

day3.name: Mission Peak
day3.analysis: Insufficient data for day 3 analysis, but the improving trend from Sunday to Monday suggests Tuesday may continue with solid thermal conditions if the NW gradient persists.

watchlist: Morning ESE offshore flow at Mussel Rock today (8–10 kts) creates rotor/lee hazard — do not launch until flow clearly shifts WNW
Strong isothermal inversion at 850mb (0°C/km lapse rate) caps thermals for all inland sites today near 4000–4500 ft MSL
Afternoon cloud surge expected at Ed Levin and Mission Peak — 76–100% cover developing after 2–3 PM, cutting thermal production early
NNW wind shift arriving at inland sites around 5–6 PM today; exit flights by 4:30 PM
Sunday (Apr 5) looks largely unflyable — 100% overcast all day at all sites with weak winds; recovery expected Monday
Monday (Apr 6) shaping up as the best day of the forecast period — Mussel Rock with solid WSW ridge flow, inland sites clearing with NW sea breeze
tldr: Today's best bet is Ed Levin high launch or Mission Peak for a mellow midday thermal session (11 AM – 3 PM) under light SSW winds with thermals capped around 4500 ft by a strong inversion — nothing epic but flyable. Mussel Rock is a no-go today due to morning ESE offshore flow and afternoon winds too light for reliable ridge lift. Sunday looks washed out under overcast; hold out for Monday when the NW gradient fills in and skies clear — that's your real fly day.
